items.hook: Ottoman wars in Europe: Siege of Belgrade: John Hunyadi, Regent of the Kingdom of Hungary, defeats Mehmet II of the Ottoman Empire.
items.body: A series of military conflicts between the Ottoman Empire and various European states took place from the Late Middle Ages up through the early 20th century. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: The Song of Achilles is a 2011 novel by American writer Madeline Miller.
items.body: Set during the Greek Heroic Age, it is a retelling of the Trojan War as told from the perspective of Patroclus. Worth a quiet trip down the rabbit hole.

items.hook: American Civil War: In the Battle of Atlanta, Confederate General John Bell Hood leads an unsuccessful attack on Union troops under General William T. Sherman on Bald Hill outside Atlanta.
items.body: The American Civil War was a civil war in the United States between the Union and the Confederacy, which was formed in 1861 by states that had seceded from the Union to preserve slavery in the United States. Bring it up somewhere. Watch the reaction.

items.hook: She was finally sunk as a target in 1970.
items.body: USS Otter (DE-210), a Buckley-class destroyer escort of the United States Navy, in service from 1944 to 1947. Bring it up somewhere. Watch the reaction.

items.hook: The 100 best novels of all time
items.body: The top 100 novels of all time published in English, as voted for by authors, critics and academics worldwide. How many have you read?

items.hook: Toronto’s expanded Platform section unveils 12 world premieres
items.body: The festival’s only competitive section offers a cash prize and potential Oscar eligibility.

items.hook: The Short Victorious War is a science fiction novel by American writer David Weber, first published in 1994.
items.body: It is the third book in the Honor Harrington series. Worth a quiet trip down the rabbit hole.

items.hook: Charles Gaines, whose book Pumping Iron helped to popularize bodybuilding, dies at 84
items.body:
1974 book led to making of seminal documentary and introduced Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world

items.hook: Ben Ladouceur is a Canadian writer, whose poetry collection Otter was a shortlisted nominee for the Lambda Literary Award for Gay Poetry at the 28th Lambda Literary Awards and won the Gerald Lampert Award in 2016.
items.body: Bring it up somewhere. Watch the reaction.

items.hook: This twisty sci-fi thriller is a fantastic debut from Isabel J. Kim
items.body: Sublimation, in which a person can split into two when crossing an international border, is tip-top stuff. Don't miss it, says science-fiction columnist Emily H. Wilson

items.hook: William Shakespeare's play, The Merchant of Venice, is entered on the Stationers' Register. By decree of Queen Elizabeth, the Stationers' Register licensed printed works, giving the Crown tight control over all published material.
items.body: William Shakespeare was an English playwright, poet and actor. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: Great Flood of 1993: Levees near Kaskaskia, Illinois rupture, forcing the entire town to evacuate by barges operated by the Army Corps of Engineers.
items.body: The Great Flood of 1993 was a flood that occurred in the Midwestern United States, along the Mississippi and Missouri rivers and their tributaries, from April to October 1993. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: Ida B: ...and Her Plans to Maximize Fun, Avoid Disaster, and (Possibly) Save the World is a 2004 children's novel written by Katherine Hannigan.
items.body: The audiobook version is narrated by Lili Taylor. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: The Soldier's Art is the eighth novel in Anthony Powell's twelve-volume masterpiece A Dance to the Music of Time, and the second in the war trilogy.
items.body: The title is from the poem by Robert Browning, Childe Roland to the Dark Tower Came, fifth line: “think first, fight afterwards – the soldier’s art.” Bring it up somewhere. Watch the reaction.

items.hook: Sunshine can help with some serious health problems, argues a new book
items.body: While Europe’s heatwave makes us run for shade, the intriguing In Defense of Sunlight reveals sunlight’s health-giving effects – in moderation, says David Robson

items.hook: Jean Charles de Menezes is killed by police as the hunt begins for the London Bombers responsible for the 7 July 2005 London bombings and the 21 July 2005 London bombings.
items.body: Jean Charles da Silva de Menezes was a Brazilian man fatally shot and wrongfully killed by the Metropolitan Police Service at Stockwell Station of the London Underground, after being mistakenly identified as one of the fugitives from the previous day's failed bombing attempts. Bring it up somewhere. Watch the reaction.

items.hook: Why open-source software can be both good and bad
items.body: Software that is free to use creates many opportunities – not all of which are good
